front: Figure: led the Free French resistance during World War II and became founder and first president of France's Fifth Republic
Event: broadcast on the BBC the day after France's capitulation to Germany, asserting "France is not alone" and urging resistance
work: World War II & the Free French

front: Event: in this speech, this leader opposed the armistice overtures made by the newly-formed government of Philippe Petain
work: World War II & the Free French

front: Figure: as a young officer, fell out with a mentor over the last chapter of a book he was ghostwriting for him, titled "The Soldier"
work: World War II & the Free French

front: Figure: after being captured at the Battle of Verdun in World War I, shared a POW cell with Mikhail Tukhachevsky at Ingolstadt
work: World War II & the Free French

front: Work: this treatise advocated for a mechanized military force and criticized reliance on the Maginot Line
back: The Army of the Future (Charles de Gaulle)
work: World War II & the Free French

front: Event: opened this conference, which granted territorial assemblies and legal equality to French colonial subjects
work: World War II & the Free French

front: Event: as head of the Provisional Government after Liberation, imposed a punishment called "national degradation" on people found guilty of "national unworthiness"
back: Purge of French collaborators (Charles de Gaulle)
work: World War II & the Free French

front: Event: in 1944, allegedly walked down the central aisle of Notre Dame while being shot at by snipers before a planned performance of a Te Deum
back: 1944 Notre Dame assassination attempt (Charles de Gaulle)
work: World War II & the Free French

front: Event: because he only led the Provisional Government and was not a legitimate head of state, he was honored with a Magnificat instead of a Te Deum after the 1944 Notre Dame incident
back: 1944 Notre Dame assassination attempt (Charles de Gaulle)
work: World War II & the Free French

front: Treaty: this agreement between France and the FLN granted Algeria its independence and was fiercely opposed by the OAS
Event: an OAS ambush sprayed this leader's Citroen DS, nicknamed "the Goddess," with bullets and blew out two of its tires
Event: this leader tersely ordered his driver to speed up while the car was under fire during this OAS assassination attempt
Figure: engineer who developed the Nord SS.10 anti-tank missile and organized the Petit-Clamart ambush
Event: this leader refused clemency to Jean Bastien-Thiry, who became the last person executed by firing squad in France
back: Execution of Jean Bastien-Thiry (Charles de Gaulle)

front: Work: this Frederick Forsyth novel was inspired by an OAS assassination attempt on Charles de Gaulle orchestrated by Raoul Salan
back: The Day of the Jackal (Frederick Forsyth)

front: Event: the Petit-Clamart plot was codenamed this, intended to avenge the failure of the Generals' Putsch
Event: an earlier OAS assassination plot was stopped at this codenamed confrontation, when radio messages coordinating men based at Bab-el-Oued were intercepted
Event: the best-known OAS assassination-plotting group was a club called this, whose members included Hungarians Gyula Sari and Lajos Marton
Event: appeared on television wearing an army uniform to help foil this 1961 uprising against him by French generals
Event: withdrew French representatives from the Hallstein Commission over a Common Agricultural Policy dispute, precipitating this crisis
Event: the Empty Chair Crisis was ultimately resolved by this 1966 agreement, which effectively granted member states a veto over EEC decisions touching vital national interests
Event: this leader twice vetoed the United Kingdom's application to join the European Economic Community, under both Harold Macmillan and Harold Wilson
Event: withdrew France's military forces from NATO's integrated command in 1966
Event: this leader's finance minister criticized the "exorbitant privilege" the United States enjoyed under the Bretton Woods system
Event: in protest of the United States' "exorbitant privilege," this leader exchanged France's dollar reserves for gold
Event: an ambassador of this leader proposed creating an organization called "the Union" in this failed 1961 plan for European political cooperation
Event: at a press conference discussing an arms embargo on Israel, this leader called the Jewish people "elite, full of themselves and domineering"
Event: clashed with Joao Goulart's Brazil over how lobsters move along the sea floor
Event: during a 1967 visit to Montreal for Expo 67, declared this four-word phrase from the city hall balcony, angering Prime Minister Lester Pearson
Figure: during a 1958 speech in an Algerian city, used a language's ambiguous inclusive "you" to claim "I understood you"
Figure: in one speech, claimed it was "Europe, from the Atlantic to the Urals" that would "decide the destiny of the world"
Figure: insisted on referring to the Soviet Union as "Russia," viewing the nation as the fundamental unit of history
Figure: enviously remarked "look at the rabble cheering the old bandit" after a visiting foreign leader received a warmer public reception than himself
Figure: once asked "how can you govern a country which has two hundred and forty-six varieties of cheese"
Figure: was derisively compared to a "surprised bathing llama" by another Western leader who had earlier undermined him during the Levant Crisis
Event: these protests began with student unrest over the occupation of the Sorbonne and involved the first nationwide wildcat strikes in France
Event: this leader fled by helicopter to the West German spa town of Baden-Baden during the May 1968 protests
Event: these agreements, which raised the minimum wage, were rejected by protesters during the May 1968 unrest
Event: upon returning from Baden-Baden, announced new elections in which his party won France's first-ever absolute parliamentary majority
Event: resigned the presidency in 1969 after losing a referendum to reform the Senate, a defeat aided by Valery Giscard d'Estaing's public campaign for a "no" vote
Figure: was succeeded as President of France by this man after resigning in 1969
Event: returned to power in 1958, ending a decade-long political retirement he called this desert-crossing metaphor
work: Return to Power & Founding the Fifth Republic

front: Event: de Gaulle's 1958 return to power came amid an Algiers uprising led by Raoul Salan and this general's Committee of Civil and Army Public Safety
work: Return to Power & Founding the Fifth Republic

front: Figure: following de Gaulle's 1958 return to power, this former Prime Minister led the Union of Democratic Forces in opposition after his own eight-month government fell
work: Return to Power & Founding the Fifth Republic
